hook: You already know that angles and distances change when you project a figure onto a different plane — a circle can become an ellipse, and a square can become a trapezoid. Yet some ratio stubbornly refuses to change no matter how you project. Four collinear points $A, B, C, D$ always carry a hidden number that every projective transformation preserves. Can you figure out what that invariant is, and how to compute it?
concept: Projective geometry studies properties that survive projection — stretching, shearing, and perspective — where ordinary distances and angles do not survive. The key invariant is the cross-ratio: given four collinear points $A, B, C, D$ (or four concurrent lines), their cross-ratio is $(A,B;C,D) = \dfrac{AC \cdot BD}{AD \cdot BC}$, where $AC$ denotes the signed length from $A$ to $C$. Because it uses a ratio of ratios, it is unchanged by any projective (perspective) transformation.
anatomy: The cross-ratio $(A,B;C,D) = \dfrac{AC \cdot BD}{AD \cdot BC}$ has four named components. The four points $A, B, C, D$ are called the base points, and their order matters critically — swapping any two changes the value. The numerator $AC \cdot BD$ pairs the two 'outer' segments of the arrangement, while the denominator $AD \cdot BC$ pairs the 'inner' segments. All lengths are signed (directed), so the sign of the cross-ratio encodes whether $C$ and $D$ separate $A$ and $B$ on the line. A cross-ratio of $-1$ is especially important: it signals a harmonic range, meaning $C$ and $D$ divide segment $AB$ internally and externally in the same ratio.

worked_example.expression_latex: A=0,\; B=4,\; C=6,\; D=10 \text{ on a number line}
worked_example.description_latex: We place four points on a number line so every signed distance is easy to read off directly. Before plugging into the formula, always list the six pairwise directed distances you will need: $AC = 6-0=6$, $BD = 10-4=6$, $AD = 10-0=10$, $BC = 6-4=2$.

We substitute the four distances into the cross-ratio formula. Notice we always put $AC \cdot BD$ in the numerator and $AD \cdot BC$ in the denominator — the memory trick is that the numerator uses the two 'skipping' distances (first-to-third and second-to-fourth), while the denominator uses the two 'spanning' distances.

We simplify the fraction to obtain the cross-ratio $\tfrac{9}{5}$. Because this is positive, $C$ and $D$ do not separate $A$ and $B$ — both $C$ and $D$ lie on the same side of the pair, which matches the picture since all four points appear in the order $A,B,C,D$.

To verify projective invariance, suppose a perspective map sends our four points to $A'=0, B'=2, C'=3, D'=5$. Computing the cross-ratio of the image points gives the same value $\tfrac{9}{5}$, confirming that the cross-ratio is truly preserved under projection — this is the whole power of the tool.

partial_example.expression_latex: A=-3,\; B=0,\; C=2,\; D=6 \text{ on a number line; find } (A,B;C,D)
partial_example.description_latex: We start by computing the four signed distances we need. Here $AC = 2-(-3)=5$, $BD = 6-0=6$, $AD = 6-(-3)=9$, and $BC = 2-0=2$. Make sure to use directed (signed) lengths measured left to right.
Now substitute the distances into the cross-ratio formula. Can you simplify this fraction completely to find the value of the cross-ratio?
The cross-ratio simplifies to $\tfrac{5}{3}$. Since it is positive, $C$ and $D$ lie on the same side relative to the pair $A,B$, which you can check visually on the number line.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Points $A=0, B=3, C=5, D=8$ lie on a number line. Compute $(A,B;C,D)$.
Four points on a line have coordinates $A=1, B=3, C=0, D=5$. Compute the cross-ratio $(A,B;C,D)$ using signed lengths.
Points $A, B, C, D$ satisfy $(A,B;C,D)=-1$ (a harmonic range). If $A=0$, $B=6$, and $C=4$, find the coordinate of $D$.
Given that $(A,B;C,D) = \lambda$, prove (or verify with an example) that $(A,B;D,C) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ by writing out both cross-ratios symbolically.
A projective transformation maps four collinear points to $A'=0, B'=1, C'=3, D'=t$. If the original cross-ratio $(A,B;C,D)=2$, find the value of $t$.

common_mistakes: Students often write the cross-ratio as $\dfrac{AC \cdot BD}{BC \cdot AD}$ but use unsigned (absolute) lengths, losing the sign. The fix is to always assign a positive direction on the line first and compute every distance as (coordinate of second point) minus (coordinate of first point), keeping negative values.

A very common error is scrambling the order of the four points in the formula after reordering them for convenience. Remember: the cross-ratio $(A,B;C,D)$ is NOT the same as $(A,C;B,D)$ — the specific pairing in numerator and denominator depends entirely on which letter is in which slot, so never silently permute the labels.

Students sometimes assume the cross-ratio is preserved under all transformations, including reflections and inversions that are not projective. The fix is to recall that cross-ratio invariance holds specifically for projective (perspective) maps; it is also preserved under Möbius transformations in the complex plane, but that is a separate (though related) fact that requires its own justification.
untested_variants: Cross-ratio of four concurrent lines (a pencil of lines through a point) rather than four collinear points, where the formula uses sines of angles instead of lengths.

Computing the cross-ratio of four points in the complex plane using the formula $(z_1,z_2;z_3,z_4) = \dfrac{(z_1-z_3)(z_2-z_4)}{(z_1-z_4)(z_2-z_3)}$ and showing it is real if and only if all four points are concyclic or collinear.
Using the harmonic conjugate construction with a complete quadrilateral to locate the fourth harmonic point geometrically, without coordinates.

Finding all 24 permutations of four points and showing they produce at most 6 distinct cross-ratio values related by $\lambda, 1-\lambda, \tfrac{1}{\lambda}, \tfrac{\lambda-1}{\lambda}, \tfrac{1}{1-\lambda}, \tfrac{\lambda}{\lambda-1}$.
